name: Production Agent Engineering
scope: A person with BASIC proficiency level in Production Agent Engineering should be able to explain what production AI agents are (LLM-driven, tool-using, automated systems), distinguish agents from simple LLM features, describe agent lifecycles (design, implementation, testing, deployment, monitoring), and understand agent roles (user-facing, internal, batch). They should know LLM fundamentals (tokens, context, temperature, model types like chat/completion, hosted/self-hosted APIs, function/tool-calling), select models based on use case, and understand limitations (hallucinations, rate limits, provider constraints). They must design and refine prompts (roles, constraints, output formats, tool usage), use prompting patterns (few-shot, step-by-step, checklists), and introduce basic guardrails (content filters, privacy, escalation). They should understand agent architectures (single-agent, sequential workflows, simple multi-agent), control flows (tool calls, branching, state handling), and when to use workflow engines or deterministic code. They must work with tools/APIs (HTTP, databases, CRMs), define and validate tool schemas, log tool usage, and use at least one framework (LangChain, LlamaIndex, semantic-kernel, cloud-native platforms) for agent and tool configuration. They should grasp retrieval (RAG basics, search/vector APIs, context construction), integrate simple retrieval, and handle privacy in knowledge sources. They must participate in evaluation (manual review, regression/rule-based checks, logging, monitoring KPIs), implement reliability/safety basics (input validation, timeouts, retries, fallbacks, access control, guardrails, incident handling), and optimize for performance/cost (token limits, model choice, latency, streaming). They should follow security/privacy practices (data classification, encryption, secret management, tenant separation, compliance basics, redaction), contribute to testing/validation (test cases, harnesses, smoke/regression tests, staging), and use frameworks/platforms for configuration, deployment, versioning, and logging. They should understand environment management (dev/test/prod, CI/CD, config management, feature flags, rollback), participate in operational support (alert response, runbooks, coordination with DevOps/SRE), and collaborate/document (agent goals, tools, prompt structures, changelogs, support docs, UX expectations, analytics signals) with product and cross-functional teams.
long_scope:
{"sections":[{"name":"Core Concepts of Production Agent Engineering","scope_included":"- Understand what a production AI agent is: an automated system that uses LLMs and tools to perform tasks reliably for end-users or other systems
- Differentiate clearly between:
  - simple LLM-backed features (single-turn completion/chat)
  - orchestrated agents (multi-step, tool-using, stateful workflows)
- Understand the typical lifecycle of a production agent:
  - problem framing and use-case definition
  - design of agent behavior and interfaces
  - implementation and integration
  - testing and safety checks
  - deployment and monitoring
  - iteration and improvement
- Know basic agent roles in products:
  - user-facing assistants (support, onboarding, guidance)
  - internal tooling agents (ops automation, data retrieval)
  - background or batch agents (summarization, enrichment)
- Understand the notion of:
  - policies (what the agent is/ isn’t allowed to do)
  - capabilities (what tools and data the agent can access)
  - constraints (latency, cost, security rules, compliance)
- Grasp the tradeoffs between:
  - fully autonomous agents vs. human-in-the-loop flows
  - generic assistants vs. narrow, task-specific agents
- Know high-level architectural positions of agents:
  - as API services in a microservice ecosystem
  - as backends for chat/web/mobile clients
  - as workflow steps in BPM or orchestration engines
- Understand basic production concerns specific to agents vs. traditional services:
  - non-determinism and variability of outputs
  - prompt and config changes as “code” that must be versioned
  - dependency on external model APIs and tool backends","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"LLM and Model Fundamentals for Agents","scope_included":"- Understand core LLM concepts relevant to agents:
  - tokens, context window, input vs. output tokens
  - temperature, top_p, max_tokens and their behavioral impact
  - system / developer / user message roles
- Understand differences between model types used in agents:
  - chat models vs. completion models
  - hosted APIs (e.g., OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini) vs. self-hosted models
- Be able to:
  - select appropriate model sizes/tiers for a given agent use case (e.g., small/fast vs. large/accurate)
  - reason about tradeoffs: speed, cost, capability, context length
- Understand basic function-calling / tool-calling concepts:
  - structured tool definitions (name, description, schema)
  - how models choose tools and produce arguments
  - synchronous tool invocation patterns
- Know high-level model limitations:
  - hallucinations and fabrication risk
  - sensitivity to phrasing and prompt ordering
  - limitations with arithmetic, long reasoning chains, and up-to-dateness
- Understand provider-specific constraints at a basic level:
  - rate limits
  - request/response size limits
  - error types (timeouts, throttling, server errors) and simple mitigation strategies
- Recognize when an agent should delegate to:
  - deterministic code (for calculations and strict logic)
  - external systems (search, databases, CRMs) via tools instead of “guessing”","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Prompting and Agent Behavior Design","scope_included":"- Understand prompts as first-class configuration for production agents (to be versioned, reviewed, and tested)
- Be able to design and refine basic prompts for agents that:
  - clearly define role, goals, and constraints
  - include style, tone, and safety instructions
  - specify required output formats (JSON, markdown, structured text)
- Understand and work with multi-part prompts:
  - system prompts
  - optional developer prompts (hidden from end-user)
  - user messages and context
- Use simple, robust prompting patterns, such as:
  - instruction + examples (few-shot prompting)
  - step-by-step reasoning prompts (without over-using chain-of-thought)
  - checklists or policies in the prompt
- Ensure prompts guide correct tool usage:
  - explain available tools and when to call each
  - instruct the model not to fabricate tool results
- Design prompts for:
  - content generation (emails, summaries, re-writes)
  - classification and tagging
  - extraction of structured data from text
  - basic planning (break down tasks into steps)
- Introduce guardrails via prompts, such as:
  - disallowed content categories
  - privacy and data handling reminders
  - when to escalate to a human or return a safe fallback
- Understand prompt failure modes:
  - prompt injection at a high level
  - instruction overriding and conflicting constraints
  - verbosity vs. conciseness tradeoffs
- Know how to iteratively improve prompts based on logs and user feedback
- Distinguish between:
  - agent policy (business rules)
  - agent persona/voice (UX and branding)
  - underlying capabilities (tools and models)","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Agent Architectures, Patterns, and Control Flow","scope_included":"- Understand basic agent architectural styles:
  - single-agent pattern (one agent orchestrating tools)
  - multi-step sequential workflows (non-branched pipelines)
  - simple multi-agent patterns (handoff between two roles) at a conceptual level
- Know common agent reasoning and tool-use patterns:
  - ReAct-style: think → act (tool) → observe → think → answer
  - planner-executor at a high level (even if using a framework abstraction)
- Implement straightforward control flows:
  - single tool-calling step with validation of arguments
  - sequential tool calls (e.g., search then fetch details)
  - simple branching based on model output labels
- Understand what “agent state” means:
  - conversation history
  - working memory (intermediate tool results)
  - request-scoped vs. user-session-scoped state
- Be able to:
  - design agents that do not require long-lived complex memory
  - clear and reset state between interactions where appropriate
- Conceptually understand more advanced patterns (without deep design authority):
  - routers / dispatchers choosing between specialized sub-agents
  - simple retries or reformulations when a tool fails
- Ensure control logic for critical decisions is not fully delegated to the model when deterministic branching is safer and simpler
- Know where to place business rules:
  - in code vs. in prompts vs. in configuration
- Recognize when a workflow engine (e.g., Temporal, Airflow, platform-specific orchestrators) might be needed vs. an inline agent loop","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Tooling, Integrations, and Environment Interaction","scope_included":"- Understand concept of “tools” / “actions” / “functions” as interfaces through which the agent interacts with systems
- Be able to work with typical tools such as:
  - HTTP/REST APIs (GET/POST, auth basics)
  - database read queries via a service layer
  - search APIs (internal search, web search)
  - internal business services (CRMs, ticketing, etc.) exposed through APIs
- Follow best practices for tool definition for agents:
  - clear, human-readable descriptions
  - strongly typed inputs and outputs where supported
  - explicit constraints and side-effect warnings (e.g., tool that changes state)
- Handle basic aspects of calling tools:
  - validate and sanitize model-generated tool parameters before use
  - handle common errors (invalid parameters, timeouts, 4xx/5xx responses)
  - log each tool call with enough context for debugging
- Know when to design:
  - read-only tools vs. write/side-effect tools
  - idempotent operations and why they matter (e.g., retries)
- Understand at a basic level:
  - latency impact of remote tools on user experience
  - the need to parallelize non-dependent calls when framework allows
- Work with at least one agent/LLM framework (e.g., LangChain, LlamaIndex, semantic-kernel, or platform-native equivalent) to:
  - register tools/actions
  - configure agents to use them
  - manage simple routing between model and tools
- Understand and respect separation of concerns:
  - business logic in services/tool backends
  - orchestration and decision-making in agents
  - presentation in client applications","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Knowledge, Context, and Retrieval (RAG-lite for Basic Level)","scope_included":"- Understand why agents need external knowledge beyond model training data:
  - up-to-date information
  - organization-specific data (policies, product docs, tickets)
- Grasp the high-level concept of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG):
  - retrieve relevant documents or snippets
  - feed them into the model as context
  - generate grounded responses based on that context
- Work with simple retrieval patterns:
  - keyword or full-text search APIs
  - basic vector retrieval through a library/platform (without designing advanced indexing strategies)
- Know essentials of context construction:
  - limit total tokens to stay within model context window
  - include citations or references where needed
  - avoid overloading the prompt with irrelevant context
- Understand the risk of hallucination when context is insufficient, and basic mitigations:
  - instruct agent to admit when it does not know
  - require references to retrieved sources when answering
- Be able to:
  - integrate an existing RAG or knowledge-lookup component into an agent
  - debug basic retrieval failures (no results, obviously irrelevant context)
- Understand privacy considerations when using knowledge sources:
  - separating private vs. public sources
  - not leaking one user’s data into another user’s responses
- Treat embeddings, indices, or search configs as part of environment configuration (even if not deeply designing them)","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Quality, Evaluation, and Observability Fundamentals","scope_included":"- Recognize that traditional unit tests are not sufficient for validating agent quality due to stochastic behavior
- Understand basic evaluation dimensions for production agents:
  - correctness / factuality
  - relevance
  - safety / harmlessness
  - usefulness / task success
  - tone and adherence to persona
- Be able to perform manual evaluation of agent outputs using:
  - predefined test prompts / scenarios
  - checklists or simple rubrics
- Contribute to setting up or maintaining simple automated checks:
  - regression suites of prompts with expected patterns (not necessarily exact matches)
  - keyword- or rule-based guards for obviously bad outputs
- Understand basic logging requirements for agents:
  - input prompts and context
  - model parameters and model version
  - tool calls (inputs, outputs, errors)
  - final responses and metadata (latency, cost)
- Work with logging/observability tools to:
  - inspect conversation traces
  - view tool call sequences
  - correlate user issues with logs
- Monitor simple KPIs for agents (even if not designing them):
  - error rates, timeout rates
  - average latency
  - cost per request or per user
  - basic user feedback metrics (thumbs up/down)
- Participate in debugging:
  - reproduce problematic interactions from logs
  - suggest prompt or minor config adjustments
  - escalate deeper issues (model choice, architecture) appropriately","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Reliability, Safety, and Guardrails (Basic Level)","scope_included":"- Understand why reliability and safety are critical for production agents:
  - user trust and brand risk
  - compliance and legal exposure
- Implement basic reliability measures:
  - input validation and sanitization before sending to models and tools
  - timeouts and retries for model and tool calls where safe
  - fallbacks: safe default messages or limited functionality when downstream systems fail
- Recognize and implement simple guardrails:
  - block disallowed content categories via prompts and rule-based filters
  - configure provider-level safety settings when available
  - simple pattern- or keyword-based checks (e.g., PII patterns, profanity) when appropriate
- Know how to apply role-appropriate access control:
  - differentiate between what an internal vs. external user agent can do
  - restrict high-risk tools to whitelisted contexts or roles
- Recognize common risk areas:
  - hallucinated instructions (the model asking user to do unsafe things)
  - hallucinated tools or capabilities
  - data leakage between tenants or users
- Understand the concept of human-in-the-loop for higher-risk decisions:
  - when to route outputs for manual review
  - when to ask user for confirmation before performing a critical action
- Follow incident handling basics:
  - know how to disable or degrade an agent feature if it behaves dangerously
  - participate in post-incident reviews (collect logs, recount behavior)
- Respect and help maintain content and usage policies defined by product, legal, or security teams","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Performance, Cost, and Basic Optimization","scope_included":"- Understand the main cost drivers for production agents:
  - input and output tokens
  - model tier (cheap vs. premium models)
  - frequency of tool and retrieval calls
- Be able to:
  - reason about approximate cost of a new interaction pattern
  - choose cheaper models for non-critical steps when acceptable
- Implement simple performance improvements:
  - trim unnecessary context and history
  - limit max_tokens where appropriate
  - avoid redundant or repeated tool calls
- Recognize latency contributors:
  - slow external tools or APIs
  - large context windows
  - multiple sequential LLM calls
- Use streaming responses where platform supports it, especially for chat UX, when directed to
- Understand the tradeoff between:
  - higher reasoning capabilities (slower, more expensive models)
  - responsiveness and cost (smaller/faster models)
- Participate in performance investigations:
  - use logs/metrics to identify slow steps
  - propose straightforward optimizations under guidance","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Security, Privacy, and Compliance Basics for Agents","scope_included":"- Understand the sensitivity of data sent to and from agents:
  - user input may contain PII or confidential information
  - tool outputs may include protected internal data
- Follow organizational policies for:
  - data classification
  - encryption in transit (HTTPS)
  - secure storage of logs and conversation history
- Handle secrets appropriately:
  - API keys and credentials for LLMs and tools must not be hard-coded
  - use environment variables or secret managers (with guidance)
- Respect tenant boundaries and authorization:
  - ensure agents only access data the calling user is authorized to see
  - avoid mixing data across customer accounts in context windows
- Understand basic compliance constraints (at a conceptual level):
  - data residency/locality where applicable
  - retention periods for logs and conversation data
  - right-to-erasure implications for stored conversations or derived artifacts
- Implement straightforward privacy-preserving behaviors:
  - minimize unnecessary logging of raw PII
  - redact or mask sensitive fields where required
- Understand risks of sending highly sensitive data to third-party model providers and follow existing guidelines for what is allowed vs. disallowed","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Testing and Validation Practices for Production Agents","scope_included":"- Participate in defining simple test cases for agents:
  - happy-path scenarios
  - common edge cases and frequent user mistakes
- Create and maintain basic test harnesses or scripts to:
  - replay fixed sets of prompts
  - compare outputs qualitatively or with lightweight rules
- Understand and apply basic test types:
  - smoke tests: agent and tools respond at all
  - sanity checks: no obviously broken or unsafe behavior
  - regression tests for known bugs or incidents
- Use test and staging environments:
  - validate new prompts, models, and tools in non-production first
  - avoid using real customer data when not allowed
- Follow simple validation patterns before shipping:
  - sign-off checklists for behavior and safety
  - peer review of prompt and config changes
- Understand limitations of deterministic assertions:
  - use pattern or semantic expectations instead of exact string matches where needed
- Help monitor post-deployment behavior:
  - watch logs and dashboards after releases
  - collect qualitative feedback from early users or internal dogfooding","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Tooling, Frameworks, and Platform Usage","scope_included":"- Be able to work day-to-day with at least one agent development framework or platform, for example (but not limited to):
  - open-source frameworks (e.g., LangChain, LlamaIndex, semantic-kernel, Haystack)
  - cloud/provider-native platforms (e.g., Azure AI Studio, Vertex AI Agents, OpenAI assistants, Anthropic tool use)
  - internal proprietary platforms with conceptually similar capabilities
- Use framework/platform features to:
  - define and configure agents
  - register and manage tools/actions
  - configure models and parameters
  - set up simple retrieval contexts or knowledge bases
- Understand the separation between:
  - platform configuration (UI/console) vs.
  - code configuration (SDKs, config files)
- Work comfortably with SDKs and APIs:
  - send chat/completion requests
  - configure tool-calling
  - parse structured responses (JSON, function arguments)
- Use basic CLI or UI utilities where available for:
  - testing prompts interactively
  - viewing logs and traces
  - deploying configuration changes under supervision
- Understand the importance of versioning:
  - keep track of prompt versions
  - maintain configuration snapshots
  - tie model and tool versions to releases
- Adhere to project conventions for organizing agent-related code and configuration","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Environments, CI/CD, and Operational Practices","scope_included":"- Understand the basic environment layout for production agents:
  - local/dev, test/staging, and production environments
- Follow deployment practices established by the team:
  - use CI/CD pipelines to deploy code and configuration
  - avoid ad-hoc changes directly in production
- Contribute to safe releases:
  - use feature flags or config toggles when rolling out new agent behaviors
  - participate in canary or phased rollouts as directed
- Understand impact of environment-specific settings on agents:
  - different API keys, endpoints, models, or tools per environment
  - different logging levels and sampling rates
- Handle configuration management for agents:
  - store prompts, model settings, and tool configs in version-controlled locations
  - use environment variables or configuration services instead of hard-coded settings
- Participate in operational support:
  - respond to alerts or incidents by checking logs
  - roll back or disable features using documented procedures
- Coordinate with DevOps/SRE where needed:
  - communicate anticipated load or traffic patterns for new agent features
  - understand rate-limiting and throttling behaviors at gateways or proxies
- Respect runbooks and standard operating procedures for common issues (e.g., model API outage, tool service downtime)","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Collaboration, Documentation, and Product Alignment","scope_included":"- Collaborate effectively with product managers, designers, and domain experts to:
  - clarify agent use cases and success criteria
  - understand user journeys and where the agent fits
  - gather domain knowledge for prompts and examples
- Document agent behavior and design at a basic but usable level:
  - describe agent goals, capabilities, and limitations
  - outline tools/actions and what they do
  - capture key prompt structures and policies
- Maintain change logs for significant updates:
  - model changes
  - major prompt revisions
  - new or removed tools
- Provide clear notes for reviewers when proposing changes:
  - what is changing and why
  - expected user-visible effects
- Participate in cross-functional reviews:
  - walk stakeholders through typical conversations or flows
  - collect and incorporate feedback into behavior improvements
- Contribute to support documentation:
  - internal runbooks for debugging common issues
  - user-facing FAQs about what the agent can and cannot do
- Communicate agent limitations honestly and help design UX mechanisms to set expectations for end users
- Work with data/analytics stakeholders to:
  - ensure logging captures necessary signals for product decisions
  - help define simple, trackable metrics for agent success","is_non_core_section":true}]}
